# **1\. Overview**

**Group-IB Digital Risk Protection (DRP)** is a brand and digital asset monitoring platform that continuously scans the internet — websites, mobile app stores, social networks, marketplaces, and instant messengers — for threats against your organization. These include phishing pages, counterfeit storefronts, impersonating social media profiles, piracy, typosquatting domains, and more.

This integration connects the Group-IB DRP portal to **Google SecOps SOAR**. It imports DRP violation events into SecOps as structured cases, surfaces URLs as actionable entities, and enables analysts to approve or reject violations directly from SecOps playbooks — without leaving the SOAR platform.

## **URLs & IP addresses for access list**

For correct API workflow, add the following addresses to your internal security access list:

**URLs:**

* drp.group-ib.com/client\_api (for API access)

* drp.group-ib.com (for web-portal access)

* sso.group-ib.com (required for interface access)

* servicedesk.group-ib.com (required for interface access)

* matrix.group-ib.com (required for interface access)

* vulnerability.group-ib.com (required for interface access)

**IP addresses:**

* 162.55.218.201

* 162.55.215.75

* 162.55.211.31

* 88.99.105.142

* 94.130.70.148

* 88.99.167.51

* 148.251.221.108

# **2\. Integration Components**

The integration consists of three connectors, four actions, and one scheduled job.

## **Connectors**

|  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **DRP Violations Connector** | Ingests all new violations as they are detected by Group-IB. Supports optional filtering by brand, section, subtype, and approve state. | None by default; configurable |
| **DRP Violations Review Connector** | Ingests only violations currently **under analyst review** (approveState \= 3). Use when your workflow requires a human decision before takedown. | approveState\[\] \= 3 (hardcoded) |
| **DRP Typosquatting Connector** | Ingests violations flagged specifically as **typosquatting** detections. | typoSquatting \= true (hardcoded) |

Each connector runs on a schedule, fetching the next batch of events from a saved sequence-number checkpoint. If no checkpoint exists, it starts from the configured **Start date** or, if unset, from 24 hours ago.

**Case model:** each connector creates **one case per violation**. If the same violation reappears in the feed after a Group-IB status update, the new alert is routed into the existing open case — provided the **DRP Deduplication Job** is configured.

**Case title:** each connector sets the rule generator to "\<Case type\>: \<host\>" (e.g. Violations: instashopify.online), making the violated host visible from the case list without opening individual cases.

## **Actions**

|  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Ping** | — | Tests API connectivity. Use during setup or to validate credentials. |
| **Get Violation Details** | DestinationURL | Fetches full violation metadata from DRP and renders it as a case insight. Outputs structured JSON for downstream playbook steps. |
| **URL-Approve** | DestinationURL | Sends an approval (takedown) request to DRP for the selected violation URL. |
| **URL-Reject** | DestinationURL | Sends a rejection (false-positive) request to DRP for the selected violation URL. |

## **Job**

|  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **DRP Deduplication Job** | 5-10 min after each run of a connector | Scans open cases tenant-wide. For any group sharing the same violation UID, reconciles younger cases with the oldest (primary) — by closing them with a back-reference comment (close mode) or merging them into the primary (merge mode, default). |

# **3\. Installation**

The installation process assumes you have access to the Content Hub. Search for **Group-IB DRP** at **Response  Integrations**, then click **Install** on the application tile. The install icon changes to a gear icon when complete.

## **Option A — Install from Content Hub (recommended)**

1. Go to **Content Hub → Response Integrations** and search for **Group-IB DRP**.

2. Click **Install** on the application tile.

3. Verify the installation at **Response → IDE → Group-IB DRP**.

![][image1]

## **Option B — Install from ZIP archive**

4. Download the latest Group-IB\_DRP\_Chronicle\_vx.x.x.zip from the Group-IB Portal Integrations page.

5. In Google SecOps SOAR, go to **Response → IDE → Import package**.

6. Select the downloaded ZIP archive and confirm.

![][image2]

# **4\. Configuration**

The configuration process has three steps: (1) enter API credentials at the app level, (2) configure connectors, and (3) configure alert mapping.

## **Before you begin**

The **GroupIB DRP** application is based on requests to the **Group-IB DRP Portal API**. Each collection has **Start date** and **Sequence update number** parameters. The sequence number is stored using siemplify.save\_timestamp() and retrieved by siemplify.fetch\_timestamp().

\> NOTE: A more detailed description of the API operation logic can be found on the API Logic Explanation page at drp.group-ib.com.

## **4.1 App configuration (Step 1\)**

7. Go to **Content Hub → Response Integrations** and click the **Gear** icon on the Group-IB DRP tile.

![][image3]

8. Insert your credentials: **API login** (your DRP Portal email) and **API key** (generated in your DRP Portal profile).

9. Click **Test** to validate connectivity — this runs the **Ping** action.

10. Click **Save**.

![][image4]

## **4.2 Connectors configuration (Step 2\)**

11. Go to **Settings → Connectors** and click **\+** to add a new Connector.

![][image5]

12. In the Connector configuration, enter your **Group-IB DRP** credentials (**API login** and **API key**).

13. Set **Case name**, **Case severity**, and **Case type** (or leave defaults).

14. Set **Start date** if needed (default: 1 day ago). Format: **YYYY-MM-DD**.

15. Choose your **Environment** and set a schedule: **Run Every** day (1 0 0 0) or 12 hours (0 12 0 0).

16. Click **Save**.

\> NOTE: The steps on the screenshot do not always coincide with the text instructions. Double-check all details in the text instructions before saving.

17. Before running the Connector in production, open the **Testing** tab → **Run connector once** and check the output. A new **Sample Alert** will appear — mark it and click **Load to system**.

\> WARNING: Go to Step 3 Alerts configuration (below) to set Mapping for the Events before proceeding.

![][image6]![][image7]

18. At the last stage, enable the connector with the **Slider** and click **Save**.

19. Check the **Logs** tab for any errors.

## **4.3 Alerts configuration (Step 3\)**

The result of running Connectors generates **Alerts** in **Google SecOps Cases**. You can add **Actions** in the **Playbooks** section to trigger them on Alert creation, apply Playbooks to Entities for enrichment, or run Actions separately for each Entity.

20. In the newly opened browser tab, click **Group-IB DRP** → **Mapping** → **Entity Fields**. This sets app-level mapping affecting all incoming Alerts. Set **Extracted Fields** as follows:

    * DestinationURL → **violation\_url**

![][image8]

21. Return to Step 2 Connectors configuration, **point 8**, and finish app configuration.

## **4.4 One-time tenant configuration (admin)**

These settings are on the **SecOps SOAR tenant itself**, not in the integration. Missing any of them can cause the integration to silently degrade (e.g. duplicate cases, deduplication job errors). Run through this checklist **before** the first connector or job runs.

|  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | **Root Cause value: \`Duplicate\`** | Settings → Case Management → Root Causes | Add Duplicate if not already present | DRP Deduplication Job in Merge Mode \= close. Without it, the job reports "The root cause: Duplicate does not exist." |
| 2 | **Close Reason value: \`Maintenance\`** | Settings → Case Management → Reasons | Add if missing (usually present by default) | Same as above — case-closure reasons are tenant-controlled. |

## **4.5 Configuration reference**

### **DRP Violations Connector**

Ingests all new DRP violations. Runs on a configurable schedule.

|  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **API login** | Email | Yes | example@group-ib.com | DRP Portal login email address. |
| **API key** | Password | Yes | — | API key generated in your DRP Portal profile. Stored encrypted. |
| **API URL** | String | Yes | https://drp.group-ib.com/client\_api/ | DRP API base URL. Must end with /. |
| **Case name** | String | Yes | Violation URL | Display name for cases created by this connector. |
| **Case type** | String | Yes | Violations | Rule generator / case type label. Used for playbook routing. |
| **Case severity** | String | Yes | Medium | Accepted: Informative, Low, Medium, High, Critical. |
| **Start date** | String | No | \*(1 day ago)\* | First-run start date (YYYY-MM-DD). No effect after the first run — connector uses saved checkpoint. |
| **PythonProcessTimeout** | Integer | Yes | 630 | Max script execution time (seconds). Do not reduce below 120. |

**Advanced filter parameters** (all optional, blank by default):

|  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Brand IDs** | String | Comma-separated brand IDs to limit ingestion. Leave blank for all brands. | Brand ID strings from your DRP portal (e.g. abc123,def456) |
| **Approve States** | String | Comma-separated approve state codes. Leave blank for all states. | 1 \= Not required, 2 \= Rejected, 3 \= Under review, 4 \= Approved |
| **Subtypes** | String | Comma-separated violation subtype IDs. Leave blank for all subtypes. | 1 Counterfeit, 2 Piracy, 3 Partner policy, 4 Trademark, 5 Malware, 6 Phishing, 7 Fraud, 8 No violation |
| **Section** | String | Single integer to limit to one platform section. Leave blank for all. | 1 Web, 2 Mobile apps, 3 Marketplace, 4 Social networks, 5 Advertising, 6 Instant messengers |

\> NOTE: When multiple comma-separated values are provided, currently only the **first value** is sent to the API. Multi-value support will be added in a future release.

### **DRP Violations Review Connector**

Ingests only violations with approveState \= Under review. The approve-state filter is server-side and not user-configurable. Use when your workflow requires an explicit analyst decision before takedown.

|  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **API login** | Email | Yes | example@group-ib.com | DRP Portal login email address. |
| **API key** | Password | Yes | — | API key generated in your DRP Portal profile. Stored encrypted. |
| **API URL** | String | Yes | https://drp.group-ib.com/client\_api/ | DRP API base URL. Must end with /. |
| **Case name** | String | Yes | Violation URL | Display name for cases created by this connector. |
| **Case type** | String | Yes | Violations | Rule generator / case type label. Used for playbook routing. |
| **Case severity** | String | Yes | Medium | Accepted: Informative, Low, Medium, High, Critical. |
| **Start date** | String | No | \*(1 day ago)\* | First-run start date (YYYY-MM-DD). No effect after the first run — connector uses saved checkpoint. |
| **PythonProcessTimeout** | Integer | Yes | 630 | Max script execution time (seconds). Do not reduce below 120. |

### **DRP Typosquatting Connector**

Ingests violations flagged as typosquatting detections. The filter is server-side and not user-configurable.

|  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **API login** | Email | Yes | example@group-ib.com | DRP Portal login email address. |
| **API key** | Password | Yes | — | API key generated in your DRP Portal profile. Stored encrypted. |
| **API URL** | String | Yes | https://drp.group-ib.com/client\_api/ | DRP API base URL. Must end with /. |
| **Case name** | String | Yes | Violation URL | Display name for cases created by this connector. |
| **Case type** | String | Yes | Violations | Rule generator / case type label. Used for playbook routing. |
| **Case severity** | String | Yes | Medium | Accepted: Informative, Low, Medium, High, Critical. |
| **Start date** | String | No | \*(1 day ago)\* | First-run start date (YYYY-MM-DD). No effect after the first run — connector uses saved checkpoint. |
| **PythonProcessTimeout** | Integer | Yes | 630 | Max script execution time (seconds). Do not reduce below 120. Do not reduce. |

### 

### 

### 

### **DRP Deduplication Job**

Scheduled safety net that runs tenant-wide to reconcile DRP cases that slipped past the alert-grouping layer.

|  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Case Type** | String | Yes | Violations | Must match the connector's Case type parameter. Accepts exact value or "\<Case Type\>: \<host\>" prefix form. |
| **Max Cases To Process** | Integer | Yes | 2000 | Safety cap on case IDs inspected per run. |
| **Lookback Days** | Integer | No | 30 | Only cases created within this many days are considered. |
| **Dry Run** | Boolean | No | true | When true, logs what would be reconciled but makes no changes. Leave on until validated. |
| **Close Root Cause** | String | No | Duplicate | Written to the closed duplicate's root cause when Merge Mode \= close. Must be an accepted value in tenant settings. |
| **Close Reason** | String | No | Maintenance | Written to the closed duplicate's reason when Merge Mode \= close. |
| **Carry Over To Primary** | Boolean | No | false | Only with Merge Mode \= close. Copies analyst artifacts (comments, tags, entities) to primary before closing duplicate. |
| **Merge Mode** | String | No | merge | close — closes duplicate with comment. merge — folds duplicate into primary via SecOps API  |

# **5\. Usage**

**Group-IB DRP** provides information on violations such as scam, phishing, malware, trademark, piracy, and counterfeit URLs. You receive the following URLs via the configured Connectors daily:

* **DRP Violations Connector** — downloads all URL types as Alerts/Entities. The created Alert name is **Violations** by default.

* **DRP Violations Review Connector** — provides the opportunity to Accept / Reject via **URL-Approve** / **URL-Reject** Actions. The created Alert name is **Violations Under Review** by default. URLs are filtered by violation.approveState \= "under\_review" and violation.status \= "detected".

## **5.1 Actions**

**Group-IB DRP** contains the following Actions:

* **URL-Approve** — mark found URL as accepted by the User.

* **URL-Reject** — mark found URL as rejected by the User.

* **Ping** — test connectivity.

To use **URL-Approve** / **URL-Reject** Actions, run them on all URL **Entities** in the **Playbook** section.

![][image9]

Or run them separately for each Entity URL — open any **Violation Under Review Alert** and click the **Gear icon** in the **Entities Highlights** section.

![][image10]

Click **Execute** in the **Manual Action** menu of the chosen Action.

## **5.2 Building a playbook for violations**

A typical violations playbook structure:

\[Trigger: Case created by DRP Violations Connector\]

  ├── Get Violation Details   ← runs on each DestinationURL entity  
  │       └── Populates entity insight panel with full metadata  
  └── \[Condition: auto-approve or send for review?\]  
          ├── High confidence → URL-Approve  (triggers takedown)  
          └── Low confidence  → Assign to analyst queue  
                  └── \[Manual task\] → URL-Approve / URL-Reject

**Steps to create this playbook:**

22. In the SOAR Playbook Editor, create a new playbook.

23. Set the trigger. Because connectors embed the violated host in the rule generator (e.g. Violations: instashopify.online), exact-match triggers will not match. Use one of:

    * **Recommended:** trigger on **Device Product** equal to Group-IB, optionally combined with rule generator **starts with** your Case Type.

    * **Alternative:** trigger on rule generator **starts with** Violations (or Violations Review, or Typosquatting).

24. Add a **Get Violation Details** step — entity type DestinationURL, scope: All entities in alert.

25. Add a condition block branching on score, subtype, or approve state from the JSON output.

26. Add **URL-Approve** or **URL-Reject** on the appropriate branches, scoped to DestinationURL entities.

27. Save and enable the playbook.

## **5.3 Approving or rejecting a violation**

### **From a playbook (automated)**

Add the **URL-Approve** or **URL-Reject** action to your playbook. The action automatically resolves the violation UID from case event data — no manual UID entry required.

### **From the case UI (manual)**

28. Open the case in the SecOps case view.

29. Select one or more DestinationURL entities in the entity panel.

30. Click **Run Action** → select **URL-Approve** or **URL-Reject**.

31. Confirm execution.

\> NOTE: The action resolves the entity URL to its DRP violation UID automatically using event data stored in the case. This works correctly only when the case was created by one of the three DRP connectors.

# **6\. Use Cases**

## **6.1 Automated phishing takedown workflow**

**Goal:** Automatically submit takedown requests for newly detected phishing pages with minimal analyst involvement.

**Setup:**

* Deploy the **DRP Violations Connector** with **Subtypes** \= 6 (Phishing).

* Build a playbook triggered by this connector.

* Add **Get Violation Details** as the first step to enrich each URL.

* Add **URL-Approve** as an automated step — this immediately submits the takedown request to Group-IB.

* Optionally add a notification step (Slack, email) to inform the team.

**Result:** Phishing URLs are automatically submitted for takedown within minutes, with no manual steps required.

## **6.2 Analyst review queue for flagged violations**

**Goal:** Route violations that require human judgment to an analyst queue before any action is taken.

**Setup:**

* Deploy the **DRP Violations Review Connector** (fetches only approveState \= Under review violations).

* Build a playbook that creates a manual task assigned to the SOC analyst queue.

* The analyst reviews the **Get Violation Details** insight and runs **URL-Approve** or **URL-Reject** from the entity panel.

* Close the case after action is taken.

**Result:** Only violations flagged for review land in the queue. Analysts have full context and can act with one click.

## **6.3 Typosquatting domain monitoring**

**Goal:** Track and respond to domains impersonating your brand through character substitution or similar techniques.

**Setup:**

* Deploy the **DRP Typosquatting Connector**.

* Build a playbook that runs **Get Violation Details** on each entity.

* Add a branching condition: if status \= detected and approveState \= under\_review, run **URL-Approve**; otherwise assign to analyst.

* Optionally add threat intel enrichment steps (WHOIS, DNS, VirusTotal).

**Result:** Typosquatting domains are ingested separately, keeping analyst queues clean.

## **6.4 Brand-scoped SOC operations**

**Goal:** Route violations for different brands to different SOC teams.

**Setup:**

* Deploy one instance of **DRP Violations Connector** per brand.

* On each instance, set **Brand IDs** to the brand's ID and a distinct **Case type** (e.g. Violations-BrandA).

* Create separate playbooks triggered by each Case type using a **starts with** match on the rule generator.

* If using the Deduplication Job, configure one instance per brand — duplicates across brands are never collapsed.

**Result:** Brand-specific violations route automatically to the right team without manual triage.

# **7\. Data filtering**

The data filtering process for Google SecOps mirrors the **Group-IB DRP** system exactly.

To explore filtering features for each collection:

* Go to the Open API Specification page on the Group-IB DRP portal (drp.group-ib.com/portal/tools/doc).

* Find the required collection in the list.

* Explore the filtering parameters shown in the window.

# **8\. Troubleshooting**

Before contacting support, review the common errors below. If the issue falls outside these cases, gather your system details, the time of the error, and application logs before reaching out.

## **Common errors — quick reference**

|  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Google SecOps can't reach Group-IB portal | Network / firewall | 8.1 |
| Group-IB token expired or not created | Token not generated or expired | 8.2 |
| Client blocks incoming data from Group-IB portal | Group-IB IP addresses not in client access list | 8.3 |

## **8.1 Google SecOps can't reach Group-IB DRP portal**

Execute the following command in your terminal (substitute your credentials):

curl 'https://drp.group-ib.com/client\_api/violation/list' \-u 'LOGIN:API\_KEY' \-H 'Accept: \*/\*' \-v

If the credentials are correct, attach the output to an Email or Service Desk ticket. For proxy environments:

* With authentication: \--proxy '\<protocol\>://\<user\>:\<password\>@\<IP\>:\<port\>'

* Without authentication: \--proxy '\<protocol\>://\<IP\>:\<port\>'

Example:

curl \--proxy https://127.0.0.1:3128 'https://drp.group-ib.com/client\_api/violation/list' \-u 'mr.demo@group-ib.com:wYM2gZ4Tc' \-H 'Accept: \*/\*' \-v

## **8.2 Group-IB token expired or not created**

32. Go to the Group-IB portal and click your username → **Profile** → **Generate API Token**

33. Click **Generate token** (or **Generate new token** if one exists).

34. Click **Save** to submit changes.

35. Open your application API profile and insert the new token.

## **8.3 Client blocks incoming data from Group-IB portal**

Ensure all URLs and IP addresses listed in Section 1 (Overview) are added to your internal access list.

## **Connector produces no cases**

|  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| No cases after first run | No violations after the Start date | Change **Start date** to an earlier date and reset the checkpoint (disable, clear timestamp, re-enable). |
| Logs say "Fetched 0 events" | Filters too narrow | Remove or broaden Brand IDs, Subtypes, Section, Approve States and re-run. |
| Logs show an API error | Invalid credentials or network issue | Run the **Ping** action to verify connectivity. Check that the API key has not expired. |
| Connector stopped producing cases | All new violations already ingested | Normal behavior — connector only fetches new events since the last checkpoint. |

## **Actions fail with "No UID found for entity"**

**Cause:** The case was not created by one of the three DRP connectors. The UID lookup reads event data stored at ingestion time. Manually added URL entities or entities from other integrations will not have this data.

**Fix:** Ensure the entity was created by a DRP connector in the same case.

## **"Get Violation Details" returns no insight**

36. Confirm the entity type is DestinationURL — the action skips all other types.

37. Check action logs for No violation UID found for entity errors.

38. Confirm the case was created by a DRP connector.

## **URL-Approve or URL-Reject has no visible effect in DRP**

* The violation's approveState is not under\_review or its status is not detected. Group-IB only processes approval changes on violations in the correct state.

* The API request was submitted but the DRP portal has a processing delay. Check the DRP Portal after a few minutes.

* Check action logs in SecOps for the raw API response.

## **Typosquatting connector times out during testing**

**Cause:** On its first run, the connector fetches all typosquatting violations from the beginning of your account history. The SOAR test mode has a 59-second timeout — this will fail.

**Fix:** This is expected. Do not test using the built-in test runner. Deploy as a live connector with PythonProcessTimeout \= 630. Subsequent runs only fetch new events and complete quickly.

## **API URL errors ("Connection failed" or HTTP 401/404)**

|  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| 401 Unauthorized | API key is incorrect or expired. Regenerate in the DRP Portal. |
| 404 Not Found | API URL is wrong. Ensure it ends with / and matches your DRP regional endpoint. |
| Connection refused / timeout | Network issue. Confirm the SOAR server can reach the DRP API host on port 443\. Check firewall rules. |

## **Cases are created with no entities**

**Cause:** Violations from the API had no fake\_uri (violating URL) populated — common for violation types where the URL is not yet confirmed.

**Fix:** Adjust **Subtypes** or **Section** filters to focus on categories that consistently include a URL.

## **Same violation creates a new case instead of updating the existing one**

**Cause:** The **DRP Deduplication Job**  is not configured, or the existing case is already closed.

* If the existing case was closed: expected SOAR behavior. A new case will be created.

* Enable the **DRP Deduplication Job** \- it reconciles open cases sharing the same violation UID.

## **DRP Deduplication Job reports "Inspected 0 DRP cases"**

**Cause:** No open cases had a rule generator matching the job's Case Type parameter.

* Confirm **Case Type** parameter matches the connector's **Case type** exactly (case-sensitive).

* For multiple connectors with distinct Case Type values, deploy one Deduplication Job instance per Case Type.

## **Cases are duplicated across connectors**

**Cause:** Multiple connectors are running simultaneously and the same violation matches more than one.

**Fix:** Choose one connector per workflow. Do not run both DRP Violations Connector and DRP Violations Review Connector simultaneously for the same account.

## **DRP Deduplication Job: "The root cause: Duplicate does not exist."**

**Cause:** The tenant does not have Duplicate as an accepted Root Cause value.

* Add Duplicate as a Root Cause in **Settings → Case Management → Root Causes** (admin required) — see Section 4.4 item \#1.

* Or override the job's Close Root Cause parameter to a value your tenant accepts (e.g. Other).

## **DRP Deduplication Job: Merge Mode 'merge' but cases:merge URL not resolved**

**Cause:** Job cannot auto-detect the merge URL for your SecOps instance.

**Fix:** Set Chronicle Instance Path explicitly. Format: 

projects/\<p\>/locations/\<l\>/instances/\<i\>. Your SecOps administrator can provide these values.

## **DRP Deduplication Job: 403 Forbidden on merge**

**Cause:** The service account lacks the IAM permission to merge cases.

**Fix:** Grant chronicle.cases.update to the service account or switch to Merge Mode \= close.

## **DRP Deduplication Job: Merged: 0 | Closed: 0 | Failed: N**

**Cause:** The job queued N duplicates but every reconciliation attempt failed. Check error lines in the log for specifics.

**Common causes (in order of frequency):**

39. Root Cause / Reason value not configured in tenant (close mode).

40. Missing IAM permission for merge (merge mode).

41. Chronicle Instance Path wrong or unresolved (merge mode).

# **9\. Support**

* For **integration issues** — contact your Group-IB account representative.

* For **DRP Portal / API questions** — refer to the DRP Portal API documentation or your Group-IB account manager.

* For **Google SecOps SOAR platform questions** (IAM, alert grouping, case-management) — contact your SecOps tenant administrator or Google Cloud support.
